title: Buss-up-shut
subtitle: Trinidadian paratha roti
excerpt: A large, buttery flatbread cooked on a tawa and beaten vigorously while hot until it shreds into irregular, flaky layers. The name is Trinidadian creole for 'burst-up shirt' — the broken, layered appearance that distinguishes it from a smooth roti. The tearing and beating is done with wooden paddles or hands immediately on coming off the tawa, while the bread is still hot enough to steam. The result is flaky, rich, and tender.
sourceNotes: Buss-up-shut (paratha roti) is a Trinidadian variation on the North Indian paratha, adapted through the Indo-Trinidadian culinary tradition. The distinctive beating technique that shreds the roti into layers is a Trinidadian development without a direct Indian equivalent. It is most often served with curry and is considered the ideal roti for scooping up wet curry sauces.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be made 2 hours ahead. Cook roti to order and beat immediately while hot.

content.text: The layers in buss-up-shut come from the fat folded into the dough. The technique is the same as laminating pastry at a much rougher scale: roll, brush with fat, fold, rest, repeat. Three to four folds produce the characteristically layered, shredded result. The beating step, done vigorously with the hands or two wooden paddles immediately off the tawa, shatters the layers apart while they are still hot and pliable.

The roti must be beaten while it is still very hot. If you wait until it has cooled even slightly, the layers will not separate and you will have a crumpled roti rather than a burst-up one. Have your hands or paddles ready before the roti comes off the pan.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Add warm water (about 170 ml) and knead to a soft, smooth dough. Rest for 30 minutes. Divide into 4 balls. Roll each ball into a thin round about 30 cm across. Spread a thin layer of softened {{unsalted-butter}} over the surface. Fold the round in half, then in half again to form a quarter. Press down and re-roll to a round again. Repeat the spreading and folding once more. Roll out the final time to about 28 cm across and 3 to 4 mm thick.

Heat a tawa or heavy pan over medium-high heat. Cook each roti for 1 to 2 minutes per side, brushing the top with {{vegetable-oil}} when you flip it. The roti is done when it is golden and blistered and the layers are visible at the edges. Immediately remove from the pan and beat vigorously from both sides with your palms or two flat wooden implements, clapping the roti between your hands repeatedly to shatter the layers. It should look dramatically shredded. Serve immediately.

items.cause: Roti too cool before beating, or fat layer too thin
items.fix: Beat the moment it comes off the pan. The layers are only pliable enough to separate when very hot. Use more butter in the layering for a more dramatic result

items.symptom: Roti stiff and not flaky at all
items.cause: Dough over-kneaded or not enough fat
items.fix: Knead minimally — just enough to bring the dough together. Each fold of fat into the dough creates the layers

items.cause: Dough too thick or tawa not hot enough
items.fix: Roll to a maximum of 4 mm. The tawa should be fully preheated on medium-high before each roti

content.text: Buss-up-shut is the roti you eat with curry in Trinidad. Its shredded, layered texture is designed to tear and scoop, soaking up the sauce of a curry duck or stew chicken in a way that sada roti, with its smooth surface, does not. In Trinidadian roti shops it is one of the standard options alongside dhalpuri, and the beating-while-hot drama of its preparation is part of the experience. The name alone (burst-up shirt) tells you everything about what it looks like and why Trinidadians love it.
